title: orders-service stuck in a retry storm after a brief DB blip
alertText:
[PagerDuty] TRIGGERED · SEV1 · orders-service
Sustained 5xx + saturation (datadog-monitor #47120)
5xx = 41% (stuck high) · incoming traffic flat ~2,000 rps but TOTAL load 9,000 rps
Retry ratio: 5% -> 78% of all requests are retries · thread pool saturated
Downstream DB latency spiked at 20:14 for ~25s then RECOVERED — yet errors persist
Env: production · Region: us-east-1
Ack: @oncall-orders · Opened 20:18 UTC
dashboardSpec: Grafana 'orders-service / health': a brief DB-latency blip at 20:14 (spikes then returns to baseline within ~25s); but 5xx jumps to ~41% and STAYS high after the DB recovers; total request rate balloons to ~9,000 rps while real incoming traffic is flat ~2,000 (the rest is retries); retry ratio climbs 5% -> 78%. The trigger is gone but the system is stuck — a self-sustaining retry storm.
logs:
20:14:02  db-primary      WARN   failover initiated (primary -> replica); brief unavailability
20:14:05  orders-service  ERROR  POST /api/v1/orders 503 : downstream db timeout (1200ms)
20:14:09  orders-service  INFO   retry policy: maxRetries=5, backoff=NONE, jitter=false (retries fire immediately)
20:14:27  db-primary      INFO   failover complete; db latency back to ~8ms (RECOVERED)
20:14:30  orders-service  WARN   incoming rps=2,010 but total rps (incl. retries)=8,940 — retry amplification ~4.4x
20:15:00  orders-service  WARN   retry ratio = 78% of all requests; thread pool active=200/200, queue=1,900
20:15:40  orders-service  ERROR  5xx = 41% and steady; new requests starve behind retried requests
20:16:10  orders-service  INFO   db latency = 9ms (healthy); errors are NOT from the DB anymore — they are from self-inflicted saturation
20:17:30  orders-service  INFO   prior restart attempt at 20:16 did not help — pods re-saturate within seconds from the retry backlog
runbookMarkdown:
# orders-service — On-call Runbook

## 1. Retry storm / metastable failure
Symptoms: a brief trigger (a downstream blip) has RECOVERED, but errors stay high; total request rate >> real incoming traffic; retry ratio is high; thread pool saturated. The system is amplifying its own load via retries and won't recover on its own. RESTARTING DOES NOT HELP — pods re-saturate from the retry backlog. Break the amplification:
- Shed load (load shedder / temporarily reject a fraction) to drain the backlog: `ordersctl loadshed enable --target 70%`.
- Fix the retry policy: exponential backoff + jitter + a retry budget: `ordersctl retry-policy set --backoff exp --jitter --max-retries 2`.
- Once the retry ratio falls and the queue drains, ramp traffic back.

## 2. Downstream DB outage (DISTRACTOR — verify first)
Applies when the DB is STILL unhealthy. Here the DB recovered at 20:14:27 (latency ~9ms); the DB is not the ongoing cause.

## 3. Deploy rollback (DISTRACTOR — verify first)
No deploy in the window; there is nothing to roll back.
groundTruthRootCause: A ~25s DB failover blip at 20:14 caused initial timeouts. Because the client retry policy uses no backoff and no jitter (maxRetries=5), failed requests retried immediately and piled up, amplifying load ~4.4x (incoming ~2,000 rps but total ~9,000 rps, retry ratio 78%) and saturating the thread pool. The DB recovered within 25s, but orders-service is now stuck in a self-sustaining retry storm (metastable failure) — the ongoing 41% 5xx is from self-inflicted saturation, not the DB. A restart does not help because pods re-saturate from the backlog.
expectedRemediation: ordersctl loadshed enable --target 70% && ordersctl retry-policy set --backoff exp --jitter --max-retries 2
expectedSlackUpdate: :rotating_light: SEV1 — orders-service stuck in a retry storm since 20:14 UTC. A 25s DB blip recovered, but no-backoff retries amplified load 4.4x (78% retries, 9k rps on 2k real) and the service is now self-saturating (5xx 41%). Restart didn't help. Action: enabling load shedding (target 70%) + switching retries to exponential backoff w/ jitter + retry budget to drain the backlog. NOT a DB or deploy issue. Next update 20:35. IC: @oncall-orders
